paper_title: AutoGUI-v2: A Comprehensive Multi-Modal GUI Functionality Understanding Benchmark

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Autonomous agents capable of navigating Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) hold the potential to revolutionize digital productivity. However, achieving true digital autonomy extends beyond reactive element matching; it necessitates a predictive mental model of interface dynamics and the ability to foresee the "digital world state" resulting from interactions. Despite the perceptual capabilities of modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs), existing benchmarks remain bifurcated (focusing either on black-box task completion or static, shallow grounding), thereby failing to assess whether agents truly comprehend the implicit functionality and transition logic of GUIs. To bridge this gap, we introduce AutoGUI-v2, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate deep GUI functionality understanding and interaction outcome prediction. We construct the benchmark using a novel VLM-human collaborative pipeline that recursively parses multi-platform screenshots into hierarchical functional regions to generate diverse evaluation tasks. Providing 2,753 tasks across six operating systems, AutoGUI-v2 rigorously tests agents on region and element-level semantics, grounding, and dynamic state prediction. Our evaluation reveals a striking dichotomy in VLMs: while open-source models fine-tuned on agent data (e.g., Qwen3-VL) excel at functional grounding, commercial models (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) dominate in functionality captioning. Crucially, all models struggle with complex interaction logic of uncommon actions, highlighting that deep functional understanding remains a significant hurdle. By systematically measuring these foundational capabilities, AutoGUI-v2 offers a new lens for advancing the next generation of GUI agents.

sections.content: Autonomous agents capable of navigating Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) hold the potential to revolutionize digital productivity. However, achieving true digital autonomy extends beyond reactive element matching; it necessitates predictive mental model of interface dynamics and the ability to foresee the digital world state resulting from interactions. Despite the perceptual capabilities of modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs), existing benchmarks remain bifurcatedfocusing either on black-box task completion or static, shallow groundingthereby failing to assess whether agents truly comprehend the implicit functionality and transition logic of GUIs. To bridge this gap, we introduce AutoGUI-v2, comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate deep GUI functionality understanding and in- * Co-authors. We construct the benchmark using novel VLM-human collaborative pipeline that recursively parses multi-platform screenshots into hierarchical functional regions to generate diverse evaluation tasks. Providing 2,753 tasks across six operating systems, AutoGUIv2 rigorously tests agents on region and element-level semantics, grounding, and dynamic state prediction. Our evaluation reveals striking dichotomy in VLMs: while open-source models fine-tuned on agent data (e.g., Qwen3VL) excel at functional grounding, commercial models (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) dominate in functionality captioning. Crucially, all models struggle with complex interaction logic of uncommon actions, highlighting that deep functional understanding remains significant hurdle. By systematically measuring these foundational capabilities, AutoGUI-v2 offers new lens for advancing the next generation of GUI agents. 1. Introduction The development of autonomous agents capable of navigating digital interfaces represents transformative frontier in artificial intelligence, promising to redefine human-computer interaction [6, 10, 1214, 16, 20, 31, 51, 52, 57, 59]. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) [5, 21, 40, 44, 47] have empowered agents to interact with GUIs across diverse platforms, effective digital autonomy requires more than reactive element grounding [15, 35, 61]. It demands profound understanding of interface dynamics, the implicit functionality of regions, and the ability to predict the digital world state resulting from an action [15]. Such predictive reasoning is hallmark of human competence and is essential for agents to generalize across diverse scenarios [1, 34, 50]. Despite this requirement, existing benchmarks largely overlook this deeper functional understanding, falling into two primary categories. The first, task-completion benchmarks [8, 26, 32, 33, 53, 63], treat the agent as black box, assessing success rates without diagnosing how the agent interprets GUI functionality. The second, grounding benchmarks [6, 15, 17, 22, 30, 54], offer fine-grained evaluation but are limited to simplistic element localization. These benchmarks typically rely on brief appearance descriptions [6, 19], alt-texts [22], or action intents [54] and fail to test the understanding of transition logic or GUI context, as illustrated in Fig. 2. While the recent AutoGUI [15] introduced functionality understanding evaluation, it remains constrained by platform diversity and lacks tasks probing transition dynamics. Consequently, critical gap remains: the field lacks benchmark that measures an agents foundational model of GUI functionality and interaction outcomes. To bridge this gap, we introduce AutoGUI-v2, comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate VLM-based GUI agents deep understanding of both element-level and region-level functionality. AutoGUI-v2 is constructed using novel VLM-human collaborative pipeline. We utilize Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking [39] to recursively divide multiplatform screenshots into hierarchical functional regions, which are subsequently verified by VLM-based scorer and refined via manual annotation to ensure high precision. Leveraging these hierarchical divisions, we generate challenging tasks that require agents to localize targets based on functional descriptions and predict interaction outcomes. Complementing this, we employ OmniParser-v2 [27] to assist in element-level grounding and captioning task generation. In total, AutoGUI-v2 comprises 2,753 evaluation tasks across six operating systems, serving as rigorous testbed for GUI functionality comprehension. Evaluation on AutoGUI-v2 reveals striking divergence in capabilities: Open-source models (e.g., Qwen3-VL) unFigure 2. AutoGUI-v2 provides rich functional semantics for both GUI elements and regions compared with existing benchmarks. expectedly outperform leading commercial models (e.g., Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) at functionality-oriented groundingthe task of localizing where function is. Conversely, these same commercial models dominate the functionality captioning task, or reasoning what region/element does. Furthermore, our analysis pinpoints why models fail. Performance plummets for irregular region types and complex interactions, indicating the models rely on overt cues and fail to grasp implicit functionality. Finally, these models are consistently tricked by hard plausible distractors (i.e., visually similar but functionally distinct candidates) in our functionality captioning tasks, proving their failures stem from lack of context-aware functionality understanding, not random error. Our contributions are summarized as follows: We introduce scalable pipeline for detecting and annotating hierarchical functional regions across multi-platform GUIs, providing valuable resource of region-level annotations to the community. We propose AutoGUI-v2, large-scale benchmark evaluating deep GUI functionality understanding at both region and element levels, moving beyond simple grounding to assessing the comprehension of GUI dynamics. We provide comprehensive analysis of leading VLMs, offering unprecedented insights into their limitations in understanding GUI functionality and state transitions. 2. Related Works 2.1. GUI Benchmarks The evolution of GUI benchmarking has progressed through three distinct paradigms. The foundation lies in grounding capability evaluation, which tests an agents ability to localize UI elements from natural language instructions; this was pioneered by ScreenSpot [6] and expanded by high-resolution and multi-application benchmarks such as ScreenSpot Pro [17], UI-Vision [30], and OSWorld-G [54]. Figure 3. Overview the AutoGUI-v2 Annotation Pipeline. (a) The process begins by sourcing diverse, multi-platform GUI screenshots. (b) VLM (Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) proposes an initial, first-level decomposition into functional regions, which are automatically scored for quality. (c) This divide-and-verify process is applied recursively, progressively breaking down complex regions into their granular, non-dividable components. (d) In the final stage, human experts provide pixel-perfect refinement of all bounding boxes, enabling final VLM pass to generate the high-fidelity functionality descriptions. Table 1. Target Type #Tasks Element Element Element Element 1280 720 3840 2160 3840 2160 1920 1080 1920 1080 Element & Region 3840 2160 3840 2160 1280 720 3840 2160 Element & Region Element Element Element 1272 1581 1581 510 8227 8000+ 3483 2000 2753 Subsequently, offline agent evaluation emerged to assess agents on static environment snapshots, exemplified by Mind2Web [8] for web, GUI Odyssey [26] for mobile, and the cross-platform GUICourse [4]. Most recently, the field has advanced to online agent evaluation in dynamic environments via tools like OSWorld [53], AndroidWorld [33], AndroidLab [56], MacOSWorld [58], and WAA [2]. However, these benchmarks typically overlook the understanding of interaction-induced state changes, failing to measure if agents grasp essential GUI dynamics. AutoGUI-v2 fills this gap by benchmarking comprehensive GUI functionality understanding. Unlike the concurrent GUI Knowledge Bench [35], which addresses interaction outcomes but lacks region-level granularity and sufficient context for element localization, our approach ensures robust evaluation of underlying GUI functionality. 2.2. Multi-Modal GUI Agents The development of multimodal GUI agents has shifted from API-dependent systems like AppAgent [62], SheetCopilot [14], and OmniParser [27] to scalable, end-toend approachessuch as CogAgent [12], ShowUI [20], UGround [10], SpiritSight [13], Aguvis [57], Jedi [55], OSAtlas [52], GUI-Xplore [37], and UI-Tars [31]. Recently, these visual methods have been augmented by reinforcement learning paradigms [23, 28, 38], which enhance generalization and reduce data reliance in complex environments. However, while agent architectures have advanced, evaluation methodologies remain fixated on task execution rates, neglecting the foundational understanding of GUI dynamics. Our AutoGUI-v2 addresses this gap by probing the underlying digital state prediction useful for agent ability [34]. 3. AutoGUI-v2 Construction This section details the AutoGUI-v2 annotation pipeline  (Fig. 3)  and task generation methodology  (Fig. 4)  . 3.1. Definitions functional region is defined as high-level grouping of individual UI elements (such as buttons, input fields, links, navigation elements, or icons) that collectively serve unified purpose. These regions are organized hierarchically, where parent regions are recursively decomposed into granular child units  (Fig. 3)  . The region types are exemplified in Figure 4. The pipeline of generating AutoGUI-v2 benchmark tasks introduced in Sec. 3.3.2 and Sec. 3.4. Fig. 1 and detailed in Sec. 6.5 of Appendix. where pscoring is the scoring prompt shown in Appendix. At the element level, we focus on visually similar elements with distinct functionality. These visually analogous yet functionally distinct components force models to rely on context rather than appearance alone. 3.2. Data Sources We aggregate GUI screenshots from diverse sources, including ScreenSpot-Pro [17], OSWorld-G [54], MMBenchGUI [48], AgentNet [49], and AMEX [3]. This multi-source strategy ensures robust coverage across desktop, web, and mobile domains (Details in Appendix). 3.3. Region-Level Functionality Understanding To construct high-quality, hierarchical annotations, we employ three-stage human-in-the-loop pipeline. 3.3.1. Hierarchical Region Annotation Pipeline Stage 1: Automated Proposal & Verification. Drawing inspiration from the AutoGUI pipeline [15], we leverage Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking [39] to automatically annotate functional regions through multi-step process. Given region image Iregion RHW 3, the VLM identifies first-level sub-regions, predicting each regions functionality {Fi} with instance-specific contextual details, layout description {Di}, normalized bounding boxes {Bi}, and divisibility flag {di}. This process is formulated as: {Fi, Di, Bi, di}K i=1 = VLM(Iregion, panno) (1) where panno is the annotation prompt shown in Appendix. Afterwards, this pipeline recursively divides the regions with di = True until all regions are no longer dividable. To mitigate bounding box errors (examples in Appendix), we incorporate refinement mechanism. separate VLM instance evaluates the cropped sub-region Ii against the root screenshot Iroot on two metrics: (1) Completeness scomp {0, 1}, checking for full visibility, and Boundedi ness sbound {0, 1}, ensuring tight framing. scomp , sbound = VLM(Iroot, Ii, pscoring) (2) i=1sbound i=1scomp For an empirically better tradeoff of cost and quality, if proposal obtains the average scores Σk /K >= 0.8 and Σk /K >= 0.8, the proposal is accepted; otherwise, the model regenerates up to = 3 iterations. Note that this mechanism is not intended for perfect division, but to provide accurate initial divisions for human correction in the next stage (More details in the appendix). Stage 2: Human-in-the-Loop Bounding Correction. To achieve benchmark-grade precision, the authors refine the automated proposals using custom web interface (details in Sec. 6.4). This tool enables annotators to traverse region trees and manually adjust bounding boxes Bi to tightly encompass the regions, correcting strictly for spatial accuracy. Stage 3: Functionality Re-annotation. Since manual spatial corrections alter the visual content of the crops, we perform final automated pass to align the semantic annotations with the refined regions. The corrected crop If ixed is fed back into the VLM to generate updated functionality ixed and descriptions Df ixed : i ixed , Df ixed = VLM(Iroot, If ixed , preanno) (3) Finally, human annotators validate these re-annotations against our quality criteria (i.e., Contextuality, High-Level Purpose, and Non-Hallucination) explained in Sec. 6.4. 3.3.2. Task Generation We generate evaluation tasks by identifying groups of visually similar regions that serve distinct functions. Stage 1: Semantic Clustering. We initialize candidate groups by computing cosine similarity matrix on the embeddings of region descriptions (Di), generated via Qwen3Embedding [64]. Regions with similarity scores ssem > thsem are clustered, effectively grouping regions that share visual attributes (e.g., layout) as described in text space. Stage 2: Group Verification and Refinement. VLM (i.e. Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) verifies these initial clusters within the global screenshot context to: 1) remove visually dissimilar false positives; 2) confirm that grouped regions possess distinct functionalities; and 3) downsample overTable 2. Stage 3: Task Construction. Using these refined similarity groups, we construct two challenge types: a) FunctionalityOriented Grounding. We generate questions requiring the agent to localize target region by outputting its bounding box based on an functionality requirement. We randomly select target region from each similarity group and generate question. For example, Which region should you interact to switch workspaces?, which strictly avoids appearancebased cues. b) Functionality-Oriented Captioning. We formulate multi-choice questions predicting interaction outcome. For example, What happens if interact with the highlighted vertical bar? Options include the ground truth, hard negatives (outcomes of visually similar regions in the same similarity group), and easy negatives (random screen functions). Answer positions are randomized to prevent bias. 3.4. Element-Level Functionality Understanding The element-level pipeline  (Fig. 4)  mirrors the region-level methodology but adapts the element discovery phase to handle fine-grained GUI components. Detection and Grouping. Unlike the recursive VLM division used for regions, we employ OmniParser-v2 [27] to detect all interactive elements. To identify visually similar candidates, we compute cosine similarity matrix on visual embeddings extracted via DINO-v3 [36]. To remove false positives, we retain pairs with high textual similarity (stext > thtext) using fuzzy matching1 and consolidate clusters via Disjoint Set Union (DSU) structure [9].. Refinement and Task Construction. The candidate groups undergo the same VLM-based verification and annotation process described in Sec. 3.3.2 to ensure functional distinctness. Finally, we generate grounding and captioning tasks using the identical formulation strategies defined in Sec. 3.3.2. These questions are tailored to element-specific interactions (e.g., double-click, long-press), challenging the model to distinguish between the target and hard negative elements that look similar but behave differently. 3.5. Dataset Statistics Our pipeline yields 3,710 hierarchical functional regions, enabling 2,753 evaluation tasks (889 region-level, 1,864 element-level). As shown in Tab. 1 and 2, the benchmark spans multiple platforms and resolutions, testing diverse, rich functional semantics  (Fig. 1)  . Further statistical details and cost analysis are provided in Appendix Sec. 6.6. Task samples can be viewed in the supplementary materials. 4. Experiments With AutoGUIv2, we rigorously evaluate whether existing VLMs can accurately understand the contextual functionality of GUI regions and elements. 4.1. Experimental Settings Evaluated Models. We evaluate comprehensive suite of commercial and open-source VLMs, categorized as follows: a) Commercial Models: Leading VLMs accessed via developer platforms, including Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking [39], OpenAI O3 [43], GPT-5 [42], and Claude-Sonnet-4.5 [41]. b) General-Purpose Open-Source: Models such as Qwen3VL [44], GLM-4.5V [46], and Step-3 [45], also accessed via cloud APIs. c) GUI-Grounding-Oriented Open-Source: Models including UI-Tars-1.5 [31] (based on Qwen2.5VL [47]), UGround [10], and OS-Atlas [52] (both based on Qwen2-VL [47]). These are deployed on an A100 GPU via HuggingFace Inference Endpoints2. All models are evaluated at their native resolutions using performance-tuned prompts (see Appendix Sec. 7). Evaluation Metrics. Grounding is measured by IoU and Grounding Center Accuracy (Acc.) [6, 15], where Acc = 1 i=1 1 (predi inside GT bboxi) 100 where 1 is an inN dicator function and the number of test samples. This metric represents the percentage of samples with the predicted points falling in the ground truth bounding boxes. For captioning tasks, we report standard multi-choice question answering accuracy [25, 60]. NID quantifies the visual clutter surrounding target etarget with bounding box Btarget (width , height H). The results are summarized in Tab. 3 and Tab. 4. Open-source VLMs specialized in GUIs dominate region grounding. Specialized open-source models demonstrate significant advantage in localizing functional regions. As shown in Tab. 3, Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct [44] and GLM4.5V [46] achieve remarkable functionality grounding accuracies of 84.4% and 84.6%, respectively. In stark contrast, general-purpose commercial models struggle significantly in this metric; for instance, GPT-5 [42] only attains 31.4%, and even the robust Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking [39] (77.6%) trails the leading open-source models. This performance gap suggests that while commercial models possess strong general capabilities, they may lack the specific ability to pinpoint large-scale GUI regions, whereas large-scale finetuning with GUI data appears advantageous. Functionality-oriented grounding presents greater challenge than description-oriented grounding. Comparing the Desc. Gnd. Acc. and Func. Gnd. Acc. columns in Tab. 3 reveals consistent performance drop across almost all models when shifting from visual descriptions to functionality. For example, UGround-V1-7B [10] drops from 88.5% to 64.7%, and OS-Atlas-Base-7B [10] drops from 72.4% to 56.3%. This trend indicates that mapping explicit visual attributes (e.g., the blue sidebar) to coordinates is inherently less challenging than deducing regions from abstract functional descriptions, which requires deeper level of semantic processing beyond mere visual matching. Standardized regions are easier to localize than Others regions. Breaking down performance by the region types exemplified in Fig. 1, Tab. 3 shows that the models consistently achieve high accuracy on standardized regions such as Primary Containers and Interaction Controls. Interestingly, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 [41] exhibits strong performance on Interaction Controls, suggesting that while it may struggle with broader regions, it retains strong capabilities in recognizing interactive widgets, such as filters, search regions, and paginations. Density preference varies by model type. The NID analysis in Tab. 3 reveals an interesting divergence. Commercial models (e.g., O3 [43], GPT-5 [42]) and GUI-specialized models (e.g., OS-Atlas-Base-7B [52]) generally exhibit their highest accuracy in Dense environments (e.g., O3 [43] achieves 61.7% in Dense vs. 17.0% in Sparse). In contrast, general-purpose open-source models (e.g., Step-3 [45], GLM-4.5V [46]) tend to perform best in Sparse scenarios. This phenomenon probably suggests that commercial and domain-specific models might benefit from the richer contextual cues provided by neighboring layout structures in dense settings, whereas general open-source models seem to prefer cleaner visual inputs with less distractor interference. For more detailed analysis of this trend, please refer to the Appendix Sec. 7. Hard negatives reveal robustness gaps in functional reasoning. To verify the rigor of our evaluation, we analyze the Error Rate by Difficulty in Tab. 4. Across all models, the error rate on Hard samples (distractors from the same similarity group) is consistently higher than on Easy samples. For instance, GPT-5 [42] has Hard error rate of 9.1% versus an Easy error rate of 2.8%. This persistent gap indicates that models are not failing randomly; they are more frequent Figure 5. Functionality understanding cases of Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking. This VLM successfully understand common GUI targets (e.g., search region) but fails on complex or stateful ones (e.g., long software status bar). to be tricked by functionally plausible distractors, highlighting that functional discrimination remains non-trivial challenge even at the region level. ately obvious. While Qwen3-VL and OpenCUA show the strongest overall grounding, their strengths are non-uniform, highlighting inconsistent mastery of action semantics. 4.2.2. Element-Level Functionality Understanding The VLMs are evaluated on our element-level tasks to assess fine-grained functional understanding. The results are presented in Tables 5, 6, and 7. Open-source VLMs lead in grounding accuracy. Tab. 5 shows that the open-source Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct [44] emerges as the top performer, surpassing leading commercial models like Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking [39]. We attribute this gap to Qwen3-VLs specialized fine-tuning on GUI agent tasks. Similarly, GUI-oriented VLMs (e.g., OpenCUA) outperform general commercial models, suggesting that largescale, domain-specific fine-tuning enhances functional understanding, though it remains insufficient to master our benchmark. Functionality-based grounding is significantly harder than appearance or intent-based grounding. To quantify the benchmarks difficulty, we test the VLMs on parallel tasks using appearance or action-intent prompts for the same elements. As shown in Tab. 5, performance on functionalitybased tasks is consistently lower across all models. This disparity, combined with higher scores on existing benchmarks (ScreenSpot-v2 [52], OSWorld-G [55]), confirms that understanding functionality is distinct and more difficult challenge than localizing based on appearance or intent cues. Models struggle with complex and implicit actions. The action-type breakdown (Tab. 6) reveals clear pattern: models perform best on actions with explicit outcomes, such as Long-Press and Type. Conversely, accuracy plummets for complex or implicit actions, particularly Right-Click (near-zero for most commercial models) and Right-Click. This suggests models fail when functionality is not immediNID analysis reveals an increasing trend. The NID breakdown (Tab. 6) shows an overall increasing trend except GLM4.5V and UI-Venus-Ground-72. For most of the models, when the number of surrounding elements increases (i.e., NID rises), the grounding accuracy for this group also increases. This suggests that element-level functionality understanding likely relies on rich context, which means determining the functionality of an element in functional group is easier than that of an isolated element. Commercial VLMs excel at captioning, revealing divergence. In sharp contrast to grounding results, top-tier commercial models (Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) dominate the functional captioning task (Tab. 7). This suggests clear divergence: GUI-specific fine-tuning improves localization (where is it?), but the broad world knowledge of large commercial models is more effective for abstractly articulating purpose (what does it do?). Captioning accuracy correlates with action types. The captioning breakdown by action type (Tab. 7) shows models perform best on actions with overt visual state changes (e.g., Type, Long-Press). Hover is consistent failure point, as its subtle feedback (e.g., tooltip) provides weaker signal for models to learn the associated function. Error analysis shows models fail on plausible distractors. Our multi-choice setup includes easy (irrelevant) and hard (visually/semantically similar) negatives. Across all models, the Hard error rate is consistently higher than the Easy rate. This demonstrates that models are not failing randomly; they are actively tricked by plausible-but-incorrect functional descriptions  (Fig. 5)  , highlighting critical gap in nuanced, context-aware reasoning. 4.2.3. Case Visualization Qualitative analysis of Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking  (Fig. 5)  reveals its capabilities and limitations. For grounding, the VLM excels at common, unambiguous elements like search bars and close buttons. However, it falters on components with complex or abstract functions, such as long status bars or the tiny, specialized icons in productivity software. For functionality captioning, the model accurately identifies outcomes for navigational elements (e.g., an email preview list) but struggles with dynamic, stateful controls. For instance, it fails to predict the behavior of application control bars or the chevron icon for collapsible folder. These cases, with more visualized in the Appendix, indicate that while VLMs grasp common functions, they currently lack robust, holistic understanding of complex GUI dynamics. 5. Conclusion We propose AutoGUI-v2, comprehensive benchmark for evaluating context-aware GUI functionality understanding at both the region and element levels. We benchmark various leading VLMs and provide insightful analysis. Our results reveal key grounding-reasoning divergence" and show that deep understanding of complex interactions and GUI dynamics remains highly challenging for current models. We hope our work will inspire future efforts to improve GUI agents. For social impacts and limitations of our work, please refer to Sec. 9 and Sec. 10.

sections.title: Datasets
sections.content: 6.1. GUI Data Sources The following GUI data sources are utilized by AutoGUIv2 to generation functional regions along with functionality descriptions: AMEX, also named Android Multi-annotation EXpo [3], is comprehensive, large-scale dataset designed for mobile GUI agents, providing 104K high-resolution screenshots from 110 popular mobile applications. As element-level functionality understanding task generation requires detecting similarity groups (Sec. 3.4), not all screenshots are qualified as task source. Therefore, we need to perform filtering before task generation. Specifically, we first utilize the data preprocessing pipeline in UIpro [16] to obtain the screenshots of the test set of this benchmark, then employ OmniParserv2 [27] to detect all elements for the screenshots, and finally find all similarity groups. Subsequently, 120 are randomly sampled from the screenshots with at least one similarity group to undergo the region division procedure in sec. 3. This approach can ensure that the source screenshots contain rich content for both region and element-level task generation. AndroidControl [18] possesses 14,548 unique tasks over 833 Android apps, allowing researchers to conduct comprehensive analysis of agent performance. Likewise, the data pre-processing pipeline provided by UIPro [16] is first used to obtain test set screenshots and filtering through similarity group calculation is subsequently performed to select 120 qualified screenshots for task generation. ScreenSpot-Pro [17] is benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate the grounding capabilities of VLMs in highresolution professional settings. This benchmark comprises high-resolution GUI screenshots from 23 applications across five industries and three operating systems (i.e., MacOS, Windows, and Linux). As the screenshots of this benchmark already contain super-rich hierarchical content, we directly detect functional regions for 271 randomly selected from the whole 1,581 screenshots. AgentNet [49] is large-scale computer-use task dataset spanning 3 operating systems (i.e., MacOS, Windows, and Ubuntu) and 200+ applications and websites. We randomly select 120 screenshots from this benchmark for functional region division. MMBenchGUI [48] is hierarchical benchmark for evaluating GUI automation agents across Windows, MacOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and Web platforms. This benchmark consists of four task typesGUI Content Understanding, Element Grounding, Task Automation, and Task Collaboration. As this data source is significantly diverse, all 1,856 screenshots are used for functional region division and task generation. OSWorld-G3 [54] is comprehensive benchmark comprising 564 annotated GUI element grounding tasks across diverse task types including text matching, element recognition, layout understanding, and precise manipulation. The 250 unique screenshots are used for region division and task generation. 6.2. Functional Region Division Details This sub-section provides detailed technical exposition of our automated hierarchical functional region annotation pipeline, as implemented in our Python script. The primary challenge, as noted in the main paper, is that VLM outputs, while semantically rich, often lack the geometric precision required for rigorous benchmark. VLM may generate bounding boxes that are shifted, incomplete, or excessively large, failing to perfectly encompass all necessary GUI elements. Our methodology is explicitly engineered to mitigate this imprecision through multi-stage, recursive propose-verifyrefine loop. The entire process is encapsulated within the FunctionalRegionAnnotator class, which orchestrates the hierarchical decomposition of GUI screenshot. 6.2.1. Algorithm: Recursive Divide-and-Verify The core of our pipeline is the annotate_image function, which implements depth-first traversal algorithm. The process begins by placing the entire screenshot (the root node, e.g., 0-0) onto processing stack. The annotator then iteratively processes nodes from this stack. Initialization: node is popped from the stack, representing region Iregion (initially, the entire GUI). to our The sent region primary Proposal Generation: Iregion annotation VLM is (Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) with annothe tation prompt panno (ANNO_PROMPT_V2_EN, shown in Tab. 9). The VLM is prompted to return list of child regions, {ri}K i=1, each with bounding box Bi, functionality Fi, description Di, and divisibility flag di. Iterative Refinement Loop: This is the most critical stage. We do not naively accept the VLMs first proposal. 3https://huggingface.co/datasets/MMInstruction/OSWorld-G Instead, we enter an iterative refinement loop (controlled by max_refine) to find the optimal division for Iregion. 6.2.2. Automated Quality Verification Within the refinement loop, each proposal (the set of child regions) is subjected to rigorous verification process using separate, high-speed checking_model (Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) and the scoring prompt pscoring (CHECK_REGION_COMPLETENESS_PROMPT, shown in Tab. 10). The full details will be delineated in Sec. 6.3. 6.2.3. Recursion, Termination, and Caching Once "best" proposal is selected, its child regions are processed: Recursion: Children ri that are marked as dividable (di = True) and are larger than minimum size threshold (its width and height are both greater than 30 pixels) are pushed onto the processing stack for further decomposition in the next iteration. Termination: branch of the hierarchy terminates when region is marked as non-dividable, falls below the size threshold, or the maximum depth (max_level) is reached. Caching: Every processed node (both parent and child) has its metadata, cropped image, and raw VLM responses saved to disk (_write_node_cache). This creates complete, auditable trace of the generation process and populates the tree.json and stack.json files, which are designed to feed the interactive web UI used for Stage 2 (Human Correction, detailed in Sec. 6.4). This automated pipeline, therefore, serves as the critical "Stage 1" of our full methodology. It is designed not to achieve perfection, but to produce high-quality, verified, and hierarchically structured "draft" annotation. This draft significantly reduces the high cost and cognitive load of the manual correction (Stage 2) and re-annotation (Stage 3) steps, forming the foundation of our scalable VLM-human collaborative system. 6.3. Functional Region Scoring Details to (the set of child regions) process loop introduced in Sec. 6.2, Within the refinement is each proposal ussubjected checking_model ing high-speed (Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking) and the scoring prompt (CHECK_REGION_COMPLETENESS_PROMPT, pscoring shown in Tab. 10). verification separate, rigorous For each proposed child ri, we assess two key metrics: 1. Completeness (scomp ): The checking model evaluates the cropped child region Ii within the context of its parent Iregion (which is marked with red rectangle for reference). It returns score from 0-3, assessing if the region is fully visible and functionally coherent. 2. Boundedness (sbound ): The model provides binary (Yes/No) judgment on whether the bounding box Bi tightly frames the functional elements without excessive padding or cropping. proposal is "accepted" if the average completeness meets our quality threshold (e.g., 2.5) = Yes) score 1 AND the ratio of "bounded" children 1 meets its threshold (e.g., 0.8). (cid:80) 1(sbound (cid:80) scomp If proposal fails, the system re-prompts the annotation VLM (with slightly higher temperature) to generate new set of regions, up to = max_refine times (e.g., = 3). If no proposal passes the thresholds, the system selects the one with the highest combined score, ensuring the best possible automated result is preserved. 6.4. Functional Region Re-Annotation Details The automated annotation pipeline (Stage 1) is designed to generate high-quality, hierarchical "draft" of the functional regions. However, We find that the VLM-generated bounding boxes lack the pixel-perfect precision required for rigorous benchmark. Stage 2 addresses this by introducing sophisticated, human-in-the-loop (HITL) correction workflow, enabled by custom web application. 6.4.1. Annotation Correction Server The core of the HITL system is FastAPI web server  (Fig. 6)  . Its primary responsibility is to serve the interactive HTML/JavaScript frontend and provide RESTful API to interface with the annotation data cache. Data Discovery: The server intelligently discovers the annotation cache by searching prioritized list of common paths (e.g., CLI arguments, environment variables, default paths). It parses the caches complex directory structure (e.g., /namespace/model/version/image_id/) to locate all processed images and their corresponding tree.json files. API Endpoints: The server exposes endpoints to securely read and write annotation data. Key endpoints include: GET /api / images: Lists all available images for correction, dynamically calculating statistics like nodes_count and corrections_count (by searching for _fix*.json files) to populate the annotators dashboard. GET /api / image / . . . / tree: Loads the tree.json file to visualize the full hierarchy. GET /api / image / . . . / node / {node _ id} : Fetches the detailed metadata for single node, merging the original VLM output with any existing correction or re-annotation files. POST /api/image/.../node/{node_id}/ correct: The critical "save" endpoint. This function, save_corrected_node, receives the new, humanverified new_bbox coordinates. Figure 6. Illustration of the functional region bounding box correction web UI of AutoGUI-v2. After detecting functional regions in Sec. 6.2, the authors deploy this web UI to inspect all annotation results, and then manually correct the bounding boxes by re-selecting the top-left and bottom-right corners. Non-Destructive Saving: To maintain full provenance and allow for easy comparison, the system employs nondestructive correction process. When an annotator saves correction, the server does not overwrite the original VLM-generated metadata. Instead, it reads the original file, updates the bbox_global and bbox_global_norm fields, adds correction_info block, and saves the result as new file with timestamped _fix suffix (e.g., 0-0_fix20251116.json). The read_node endpoint is designed to find and load this "fixed" version if it exists. 6.4.2. Interactive Correction Interface The frontend is single-page application (SPA) designed for maximum annotator efficiency. It features three-panel layout: 1. Image List: searchable list of all GUIs, with status indicators showing the percentage of nodes that have been corrected. 2. Hierarchy Panel: D3.js-based interactive tree visualization of the selected GUIs full functional hierarchy. Clicking node in this tree loads its metadata in the details panel. 3. Full Image Panel: view of the entire root screenshot with all VLM-proposed bounding boxes rendered on an SVG overlay. 6.4.3. Convenient Bounding Box Correction Workflow The correction process is initiated when an annotator selects node and opens the "Fix BBox" modal. This interface is equipped with advanced tools to make correction fast and precise: Full-Context View: The modal displays the full root image, not the small region crop. This provides the annotator with complete context to make an accurate judgment about the regions true boundaries. Sharpened Zoom Preview: real-time, magnified preview follows the annotators cursor. This feature uses OpenCV.js to draw the relevant portion of the image onto canvas and applies 2D sharpening filter (cv.filter2D). This enhancement makes it significantly easier to identify precise UI element borders. Auto-Snap to Edges (Long-Press): For rapid, perfect alignment, the annotator can long-press. This triggers our "auto-snap" feature, which uses OpenCV.js to perform Canny edge detection (cv.Canny) and probabilistic Hough line transform (cv.HoughLinesP) in local window around the cursor. The system then calculates the closest point on the nearest detected line and snaps the coordinate to it. Auto-Snap to Corners (Ctrl + Long-Press): For perfect corner alignment, annotators can use Ctrl + long-press. This triggers an alternative snap mode that uses the "Good Features to Track" algorithm (cv.goodFeaturesToTrack) to find strong Harris corners in the local window, snapping the cursor to the nearest detected corner. When the annotator confirms the new coordinates, the UI POSTs the data to the server, which saves the nondestructive file. The UI then automatically refreshes the image card and node details, providing immediate feedback on the correction. 6.4.4. Functionality Re-Annotation The human-in-the-loop correction guarantees the geometric precision of our bounding boxes. However, this necessary step creates semantic misalignment: the original, VLMgenerated descriptions (Fi, Di) from Stage 1 now correspond to different, imprecise crop. Stage 3 is the critical final pass designed to re-align the semantic annotations with the new, human-verified geometry. This is achieved by prompting Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking to revise the annotations based on the corrected bounding boxes. The re-annotation process is orchestrated by batch script. This script does not re-process the entire dataset, but only the nodes that have been manually corrected. 1. Correction Discovery: The re-annotating script recursively scans the cache directory. It searches for all humancorrected metadata files, which are identified by their _fix*.json or _meta_fix*.json suffix. 2. Task Queuing: For each corrected file found, the script generates CorrectionTask object. This object contains paths to the original root image, the nodes original metadata, and the human-corrected file. core is functionality this specialized 3. Parallel Execution: The script processes this queue in parallel using multiprocessing.Pool. Each worker executes the core reannotate_node function for given task. reof The VLM prompt annotation (REANNOTATION_PROMPT_TEMPLATE in shown Tab. 11) that provides the model with rich, multi-modal context to perform an expert revision. For each node, the reannotate_node function prepares the following inputs: The Full-Screen Context: The original root.png is loaded, and the new, human-verified bounding box (from the _fix*.json file) is drawn onto it as conspicuous red rectangle. Original Flawed previous_functionality The Corrected Crop: The script uses the human-verified coordinates to crop the root.png again, producing new, geometrically-accurate image of the functional region. The The previous_description the original node metadata. The VLM is then prompted to act as "UI/UX analyst" and is explicitly told that human has corrected the bounding box, implying the previous annotations may be "incorrect, either containing hallucinated details or missing important discernible details." The VLM is instructed to ignore the previous text if it conflicts with the new visual evidence and to revise the functionality and description. Annotations: and from extracted are 6.4.5. Non-Destructive Output The VLMs the system is nonTo maintain full data provenance, destructive. JSON response, which includes the revised functionality, revised description, and revision rationale, is not used to overwrite any existing files. is (e.g., {node _ saved as new, separate artifact, id } _ meta _ reannotated _ gemini - 2 . 5 - pro - thinking.json). Instead, it This final file represents the culmination of our pipeline: functional region defined by human-verified geometry and VLM-revised semantics, creating high-fidelity data point for our benchmark. 6.5. Functional Region Classification Details The previous stages establish the geometric boundaries and functional descriptions of each region. However, to enable granular analysis and stratified evaluation (as seen in the experiments section, i.e., Sec. 4), each region must also be categorized into standardized taxonomy (e.g., "Navigation Bar," "Modal," "Data Table"). This stage automates this classification process using Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking, ensuring consistency across the diverse GUI layouts in our dataset. 6.5.1. Taxonomy Definition We define comprehensive, hierarchical taxonomy tailored for modern GUIs, covering 10 high-level categories and over 40 specific types, shown in Tab. 8. This taxonomy is embedded directly into the Python script (TAXONOMY constant) and includes definitions such as: Primary Interface Containers: Application Window, Browser Tab, Split-Screen Pane. Global Navigation: Header/Top Bar, Sidebar, Tab Bar, Breadcrumbs. Content Display: Main Content Area, Card List, Data Table, Dashboard Widget. Interaction & Input: Search Region, Form, Filter Controls, Pagination. Contextual Elements: Modal/Dialog, Popover, Dropdown Menu, Notification Banner. For leaf nodes (individual elements), the taxonomy is extended with types like "Button," "Link," and "Fragmented Element" to handle fine-grained classification. 6.5.2. Classification Pipeline The classification is performed by dedicated script (classify_functional_regions.py) that iterates through all annotated regions. The process for each region is as follows: 1. Filtering: The script first filters out the root node ("Entire GUI") and any nodes that do not meet minimal size criteria, as these are trivial or non-informative. 2. Context-Aware Prompting: The VLM is presented with the cropped image of the region. To ensure accuracy, the prompt (build_classification_prompt) provides the model with: The full taxonomy list with definitions. set of few-shot examples demonstrating correct classification (e.g., distinguishing an "Application Window" containing dialog from the "Modal" itself). Instructions to select the single best fitting type or use "Other" with generated subtype if necessary. 3. Robust Parsing: The VLMs response is parsed to extract the type, subtype, and confidence score. The system includes robust regex-based fallback mechanisms to handle varied VLM output formats. 4. Inheritance Logic: For regions that are not explicitly classified (e.g., intermediate grouping nodes), the system implements fallback inheritance logic (get_inherited_type), allowing them to adopt the classification of their parent or child nodes where appropriate to maintain semantic continuity in the hierarchy. 6.5.3. Output Integration The classification results are saved as separate JSON artifacts (e.g., [node_id]_region-type.json) alongside the node metadata. This modular approach allows the classification layer to be updated or refined independently of the core annotation data. These types are subsequently used to generate the breakdown statistics in our experimental results, allowing us to analyze model performance across different GUI components (e.g., distinguishing performance on "Navigation" vs. "Content" regions). 6.6. More Dataset Statistics 6.6.1. Cost Analysis Functional Region Division Cost. The functional region division stage recursively detects functional regions, so accurate cost calculation is nearly infeasible. To provide an estimate, we simply consider the input size to always be 1920 1080. Using Gemini-2.5-Pro to detect the functional regions for the root image (1920 1080), the average number of input tokens Ninput = 4500 plus that of output tokens Noutput = 4000 leads to 4500/1000000 1.25 + 4000/100000010 = 0.045625 dollars4. If the maximum of 10 regions are detected at the 2nd level, the estimated cost detection is 10(3000/10000001.25+4000/100000010) = 0.4375. Regional Refinement Cost. Assuming one detected functional region is 1/10 of the size of the root image (1920 1080), the cost of one round of refinement with an average input tokens 1450 and output tokens 480 is 480/1000000 1.25 + 1450/1000000 10 0.0151 dollars. If the maximum repeat of refinement 3 is reached, then 10 regions cost 3 10 0.0151 0.453 dollars. Re-Annotation Cost. Still, assuming one detected functional region is 1/10 of the size of the root image (1920 1080), the cost of re-annotating region with an average input tokens 1080 and output tokens 1680 is 1080/1000000 1.25 + 1680/1000000 10 0.01815 dollars. Then 10 regions cost 10 0.01815 0.1815 dollars. The total cost is estimated to be 0.4375 + 0.453 + 0.1815 = 1.072 dollars for processing one screenshot of size 1920 1080. Table 8. The number of the action types involved in the functionality-based element grounding tasks generated in Sec. 3.4. 6.6.2. Task Statistics The proportions of the region types are listed in Tab. 8. The task attributes (i.e., the action type involved, density class, and the size of the similarity group the target belongs to) are illustrated in Fig. 7, Fig. 8, Fig. 9, and Fig. 10. 6.7. Similar Functional Region Grouping Details To ensure that the generated evaluation tasks rigorously test an agents ability to distinguish functionality rather than relying on superficial visual matching, we construct groups of visually similar but functionally distinct regions. This grouping process involves cascade of semantic embedding, VLM-based visual verification, and deterministic geometric refinement. 4The Gemini-2.5-Pro pricing is listed at https://ai.google. dev/gemini-api/docs/pricing#gemini-2.5-pro Figure 8. The proportions of the density classes and the similarity group sizes of the functionality-based element grounding tasks generated in Sec. 3.4. 6.7.1. Initial Semantic Grouping. We utilize Qwen3-Embedding to compute embedding vectors for the visual descriptions of all functional regions. An affinity matrix is constructed based on cosine similarity. Regions are clustered into initial groups if their similarity exceeds threshold of 0.6. To ensure the independence of elements within group, we strictly filter out any pairs that exhibit parent-child relationship using the hierarchy defined in tree.json. Furthermore, only regions that have undergone manual bounding box correction are considered for grouping to guarantee geometric precision. 6.7.2. Visual Verification by VLM. Semantic embeddings of text descriptions may miss subtle visual nuances. Therefore, we employ Gemini-2.5Pro-Thinking to visually inspect the initial groups against the original screenshot. The model performs three critical checks: (1) confirming that grouped elements are visually 4. Duplicate Merging: We identify groups that share common elements. If two groups share 2 elements, they are merged into single cluster. During this merger, we re-apply the parent-child filter to remove any hierarchical conflicts introduced by the combination. 6.7.4. Refinement of Abnormal Groups. Groups flagged as oversized or those resulting from the merging process undergo second pass processing by Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking. In this step, the model is tasked with selecting the optimal subset of elements (specifically targeting size of 2 to 5) that best represents the visual pattern while maximizing functional confusion. Crucially, this step is subtractive only; the model can remove elements to refine the group but is not permitted to add new ones. 6.7.5. Final Validation. final Python-based validation is performed to ensure strict adherence to benchmark standards. We re-verify that no bounding box overlaps exist and that the final group size is strictly between 2 and 5 elements. Only groups passing these final constraints are used for task generation. 7. Extra Evaluation Details 7.1. Functionality-Based Region Grounding Failure

sections.title: Case Inspection
sections.content: In the grounding task, distinct failure patterns emerged that highlight trade-off between semantic reasoning and localization precision. Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking demonstrates strong reasoning capabilities, often correctly identifying the target UI element corresponding to the functional description and locating the rough area. However, as shown in Fig. 11, its failure cases are predominantly characterized by spatial imprecision, where the predicted bounding boxes exhibit low IoU with the Ground Truth. The generated boxes are frequently too loose or significantly shifted, despite covering the correct semantic region. Conversely, Qwen3-VL-32BInstruct displays superior capabilities in bounding box regression, producing tight and geometrically accurate boxes. However, it falters significantly in target identification, frequently predicting high-confidence bounding boxes for the wrong elements. This indicates that while Gemini struggles with fine-grained pixel-level regression, Qwen tends to prioritize visual saliency over strict alignment with the functional textual description. 7.2. Functionality-Based Region Captioning Failure Case Inspection For the captioning task, the error distribution reveals shared deficiency in understanding the hierarchical structure of user interfaces. The primary failure mode for both Gemini-2.5Pro-Thinking and Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct falls into two Figure 9. The number of the action types involved in the functionality-based element captioning tasks generated in Sec. 3.4. Figure 10. The proportions of the density classes and the similarity group sizes of the functionality-based element captioning tasks generated in Sec. 3.4. similar (e.g., in shape, color, or icon style) but perform different functions; (2) identifying potentially missing candidates from provided list of other elements and adding them to the group, strictly ensuring that any added element does not overlap spatially (bounding box intersection) with existing group members; and (3) ensuring the group size remains within reasonable range (2 to 5 elements). 6.7.3. Geometric and Topological Processing. Following the visual verification, we apply series of deterministic checks and refinements in Python: 1. Overlap Resolution: We iteratively check for bounding box overlaps within each group. If an overlap is detected, one element (typically the one with the larger area) is removed, and the check is repeated until no overlaps remain. 2. Minimum Size Check: Groups that have been reduced to fewer than 2 elements after verification or cleanup are immediately discarded. 3. Oversized Group Detection: Groups containing more than 5 elements are flagged as oversized. Figure 11. Visualization of typical failure cases by Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking on the functionality-based region grounding task. Figure 12. Visualization of typical failure cases by Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking on the functionality-based region captioning task. main categories: differentiating between "System vs. Application" layers (e.g., confusing status bars with app-specific headers) and distinguishing different functionalities within "Single Application" (e.g., misinterpreting similar icons within the same app context). Fig. 12 provides detailed visualization of these failure patterns observed in Gemini. In addition to these shared errors, Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct exhibits sporadic instances of "System vs. System" and "Inter-App" confusions. Collectively, these failure cases validate our hypothesis that existing models still lack robust capability to predict and distinguish complex GUI functionalities, relying heavily on surface-level visual features rather than deep interactive logic. 7.3. Functionality-Based Element Grounding Failure Case Inspection To further investigate how the VLMs fail in the functionalitybased element grounding, we manually inspected 30 cases and found that Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking owns the capability of pinpointing the target element in its reasoning content, but it cannot correctly predict the bounding box coordinates. For example, the reasoning content demonstrates redundant numeric computation about the four components of box coordinate but is totally incorrect and useless. 90% of cases are due to incorrect reasoning, while 10% are due to misunderstanding of functionality. Typical incorrect cases are visualized in Fig. 13. 7.4. Functionality-Based Element Captioning Failure Case Inspection To further investigate how the VLMs fail in the functionalitybased element captioning, we manually also inspected 30 cases and found that Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking also performs poorly. For example, Fig. 14 shows that this model fails to predict the outcome of clicking the app icon on the system tray at the bottom, mistakenly believing that this interaction just returns to the previous screen with the app. However, the real outcome is that the entire app window will be minimized. Moreover, this model also fails to distinguish the different alignment buttons in the Linux spreadsheet software. These examples indicate that even powerful VLM like Gemini2.5-Pro lacks satisfactory GUI functionality understanding capability. Figure 13. Visualization of typical failure cases by Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking on the functionality-based element grounding task. Figure 14. Visualization of typical failure cases by Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking on the functionality-based element captioning task. 8. Prompt Suites 8.1. AutoGUI-v2 Annotating Prompts The prompts used by AutoGUI-v2 annotation pipeline are listed in Tab. 9, Tab. 10, and Tab. 11. Table 9. The prompt used to instruct Gemini-2.5-Pro-Thinking to perform hierarchical GUI functional region division. Input Prompt Template <image> You are an expert UI/UX analyst. Your task is to analyze graphical user interface (GUI) screenshot and identify all functional regions. functional region is high-level grouping of individual UI elements (such as buttons, input fields, links, navigation elements, or icons) that collectively serve single, overarching purpose. Instructions: 1. Identify All First-Level Functional Regions: Scrutinize the entire GUI to identify every distinct, interactive region, including navigation bars, user profile management region, search regions, detailed content regions, sidebars, app windows, and other interactive regions. 2. Determine Functionality: For each identified region, first describe its visual layout and then deduce its primary functionality in detail. - 2.1. Provide high-level description of the region/elements function. Avoid detailing every specific functionality. Instead, focus on its broader impact on the webpage experience. For example, if interacting with "Products" region reveals dropdown menu, do not catalog the subsequent webpage changes in exhaustive detail. - 2.2. To ensure uniqueness, your functionality description should reflect the instance-specific context of the region whenever possible. For example, instead of predicting This region is used to search, you should predict This region allows users to search for electronic products on Amazon, where electronic products on Amazon is specific to the current instance. Similarly, rather than predicting This element facilitates the selection of an hour for the return time, you should predict This element updates the return time to 13 p.m on the clock picker. if such information is directly available. Ensure that the description remains accurate, grounded in visible data, and does not speculate on unseen values. 3. Generate Normalized Bounding Boxes: For each region, provide the precise bounding box coordinates in normalized format [xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax] . The coordinates must be between 0 and 1000, where (xmin, ymin) is the top-left corner and (xmax, ymax) is the bottom-right corner of the region. All boxes should tightly bound the region and MUST not overlap. 4. Be Thorough: Identify all non-overlapping regions. Skip purely decorative or non-interactive elements like static text, background images, or simple separators. 5. Division Limit: The number of the detected functional regions should be less than 10 as too many regions may indicate that you accidentally break down large region into too many small regions. 6. Determine Dividability: For each region, determine whether it can be divided into smaller lower-level self-contained functional regions. If yes, set the "dividable" field to true; if no, set it to false. 7. Input Prompt Template <image> You are meticulous AI assistant specializing in visual analysis and quality control. Your task is to evaluate cropped image depicting functional region within larger GUI screenshot and determine the completeness and boundedness of the shown region, providing quantifiable score. In images with multiple overlapping windows, the primary application window in focus should be considered the main functional region. region box is considered valid if it meets both of the following criteria: - Completeness: This measures how much of the functional region is visible and unobstructed within the provided boundaries. region is considered incomplete if its key components are obscured or occluded by other overlapping elements (like pop-up dialogs or other windows). This score is independent of the boxs framing. - Boundedness: This measures the quality of the framing. region is poorly bounded if the box is either: (a) Too tight: The box crops, clips, or cuts off any part of the functional region. Too loose: (b) The box includes excessive, non-functional padding or background area that is not part of the subject. Your Task Analyze the user-provided image and its highlighted region. Then, provide your assessment in the specified format. Scoring Rubrics A. Completeness Score (Evaluates Occlusion): 3 (Perfect): The functional region is fully visible within the box. No part of it is occluded by other elements. 2 (Almost Complete): Only minor, non-critical parts of the region are occluded. 1 (Mostly Incomplete): large or critical part of the region is occluded. 0 (Severely Incomplete): The vast majority of the functional region is occluded or missing. B. Boundedness (Evaluates Framing): (a) Yes: The box tightly frames the entire functional region without cropping it and without including excessive padding. (b) No: The box either crops the functional region or includes excessive padding. Critical Rule: If region is cropped by its boundary (Boundedness: No), its Completeness Score cannot be 3, because cropped view is inherently an incomplete one. Societal Impact The advancement of VLM-based GUI agents enables significant accessibility benefits but introduces dual-use risks regarding labor displacement and digital security. We address these dimensions below: Labor and Surveillance. While AutoGUI-v2 aims to reduce cognitive load via automation, robust GUI agents could theoretically facilitate workplace surveillance or displace roles in data entry and QA. We argue that the ethical deployment of such agents requires "human-in-the-loop" paradigm, where the agent functions as an augmenting copilot rather than an autonomous replacement. Data Ethics and Bias. Our dataset is constructed from open-source repositories and public interfaces, rigorously scrubbed for offensive content. We explicitly acknowledge distributional bias toward English-language, Western-centric design patterns. Consequently, agents evaluated solely on this benchmark may lack cross-cultural generalization, and we caution against assuming universal applicability without further diverse testing. 10. Limitations and Future Work AutoGUI-v2 provides semi-automated GUI functional region discovery pipeline and comprehensive functionality understanding benchmark. Nevertheless, AutoGUI-v2 faces the following limitations: Full automation is not yet achieved. The AutoGUI-v2 annotation system requires human annotators to correct the bounding boxes of the detected functional regions. This human-in-the-loop annotation procedure restricts the scaling potential of AutoGUI-v2. Future work can explore reliable self-verification that substitutes human labor with competent model assistance. Lack of task relevance. The AutoGUI-v2 possesses massive functionality and appearance descriptions for the detected functional regions.
